description: Apollo 13 Flight Journal - Day 5, part 2: Feeling the Cold

tokens: Strange noises in the Descent Stage and battery alarms have been the woe of the crew of Apollo 13 for the past few hours.
Now over 100 hours into their mission, another 40 hours in space remain until they are to splash down onto the Pacific Ocean.

They have also been provided with instructions on how to perform a mostly manually controlled course correction burn using the AGS backup guidance system and visual references by looking out of the LM window.
Plans also exist for charging the somewhat depleted CM reentry batteries from LM power using the cables that would normally supply power from the CM into the LM.
Their stores of power, oxygen and water are still Go, but subject to careful rationing.
Another worry is the constantly dropping temperature, not only uncomfortable to the crew, but a potential threat to their equipment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, Jim, we have a lengthy procedure here for powering up the CSM and turning on instrumentation so we can check the TM, and this will take a large piece of scratch paper whenever you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: This is to power up the CSM?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I'll tell you what, I'll have Jack get configured.
He can copy that down and I'll have him ...
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Okay, Vance, how do you read the Aquarius?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, read you loud and clear, Aquarius.
Is it cool in there now?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: This is the third officer on this LM crew here.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Jack has been mostly unoccupied since the accident, considering he has little expertise in the LM systems or its operation.
His workload is about to increase considerably now that the return to the Command Module approaches.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jack, this is a lengthy procedure, take probably two or three pages.
It assumes that you are in your nominal configuration, which was sent up ...
or in your day-flying configuration, which was sent up to you earlier today.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The original procedure is provided from the links below as available.
What they want to do is to switch on the Command Module batteries and power up some of the onboard system to see how they have survived since the Command Module was powered down after the accident.
This will give everyone involved information on what kind of procedures might be needed for their reentry if there is evidence of the systems degrading from the cold temperature.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The LMP's side of the cabin has several circuit breaker panels that Jack is now about to use to get power from the batteries and into the systems onboard the dormant Odyssey.
You can view a version with the panels annotated for clarityfrom this link.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I can verify that we are in that configuration with the exception of panel 382, the water accumulator, which I've left off in case we wanted to get some more drinking water out of the Command Module, and I'll put those in the proper configuration before we do anything.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Panel 5: close the following circuit breakers: ECS, Press Group 1, Main B; ECS, Press Group 2, Main B; ECS, Temp, Main B; ECS, Secondary Loop Transducer, Main B; ECS Rad, Control/Heaters, Main B; Bat Relay Bus, Bat B; Bat Charger, Bat B, Charge ...

or to B, Charge; Inverter Control, 2; Inverter Control, 1; EPS Sensor Signal, AC1; EPS Sensor Signal, Main B; EPS Sensor Unit, AC Bus 1; Waste/Potable Water, Main B; Instruments, ESS, Main B; that's Essential, Main B.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jack's first step is to work the LMP's side console in the Command Module and close circuit breakers that supply power into the various sensors in the main systems that they want to check out here.
They will also prepare the inverters for producing the AC power required by the telemetry sensors .
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Why don't you read that group back, and then we'll proceed on.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Because I don't know where I ...
Okay, panel 4; Telcomm, Group 1, to AC1.

On panel 5, close the following circuit breakers: ECS, Pressure Group 1, Main B; ECS, Pressure Group 2, Main B; ECS, Temp, Main B; ECS, Secondary Loop Transducer, Main B; ECS Rad, Control/Heaters, Main B; Bat Relay Bus, Bat B; Bat Charger, Bat B; Inverter Control, 2; Inverter Control, 1; EPS Sensor Signal, AC1; EPS Sensor Signal, Main B; EPS Sensor Unit, AC1; Waste/Potable H2O, Main B; Instrumentation, Essential, Main B.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Is that reading rate okay for you?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Okay, and leave a little space if you can to the right of these because when we talk about the backup procedure, why then we can just use the same listing, and I'll ...
I'll tell you open instead of close these circuit breakers, or at least most of them.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Next, panel 3: Transponder, Primary; Power Ampl, to Primary, that's a verify; Power Ampl, High; Mode, Voice, to Off; Power, SCE, to Normal; PMP to Normal; S-Band Antenna, Omni D, that's Delta.
Okay, why don't you read that one back?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: On panel 3: Transponder to Primary; Power Amplifier to Primary; Power Ampl, High; Mode, Voice, to Off; Power, SCE, to Normal; PMP to Normal; S-Band Antenna, Omni D, D as in Doggy.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The next step starts up the Command Module's communication system and configures it to produce a high power signal for the purpose of transmitting telemetry to Mission Control.
They will use the Omni D antenna for this.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, panel 225: circuit breaker Flight Bus, Main B, to close; circuit breaker CTE, Main B, to close.
Panel 250: CB Bat C Bat Charger/EDS 2 to close.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Next they close circuit breakers that provide power to the aforementioned communications equipment, as well as the CTE - Central Timing Equipment.
The CTE provides timing reference used by many of the onboard systems to synchronize them.

While the Command Module Computer usually provides the basic timing reference, the CTE has its own crystal-based oscillator - not unlike a quartz clock - to provide this function when the computer is not available.
This separate panel is under the Main Display Console.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On panel 225: CB Flight Bus, Main B, close; and then I lost you after that.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: After that, CB CTE, Main B, to close.
Next, panel 250: CB Bat C Bat Charge/EDS2 to close; CB Bat C Power Entry/Postlanding to close.
Next, panel 275: CB Main B, Bat Bus B, to close; CB Inverter Power 2, Main B, to close.
Incidentally, anything that I've given you up until now is not sequence critical, but from now on, it should be ...
you should be sure to do it in the sequence we give you.
Why don't you read that back, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Panel 250 connects the Command Module batteries to the power distribution system.
Now they're connecting Battery C in.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Let me give you all of panel 225 again.
CB Flight Bus, Main B, close; CB CTE, Main B, close.
B Bat Charge, that should be Bat C, Charlie, isn't it?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Bat Charlie Bat Charge/EDS2, close; Bat Baker Power Entry/Postlanding, closed.
On panel 275: CB Main B, Bat Bus B, closed; CB Inverter Power 2, Main B, closed.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: These switches are used to connect both the battery and Power Inverter 2 to Main Bus B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's correct except for the second circuit breaker on panel 250, which is CB Bat Charlie Power Entry slash Postlanding, close.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: CB Bat Bravo Power Entry/Postlanding, close.
Next, MDC 3, AC Inverter 2 to Main B.
AC Inverter 2, AC Bus 1, on up.
Next, panel 5: AC Inverter AC Bus 1 to Reset and then center.
Panel 3: Up Tlm to Command Reset then Off.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The following steps are sequence critical.
Panel 250: CB Bat B Power Entry/Postlanding, closed.
MDC 3, AC Inverter 2, to Main B; AC Inverter 2, AC1, on [Garbled].
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: After pushing in the breaker on Panel 250 to connect Battery B into the system, they will then power the AC Inverter number 2 from Main Bus B, and connect Inverter 2 to AC Bus 1 to provide AC power for the telemetry sensors.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: How do you read now, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: did you copy any of my steps there?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, I heard you through AC Inverter 2, AC Bus 1 to on, and you were cut out by noise.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Okay, Now comes to one I didn't understand.
You said go over to MDC 5 and A in ...
AC Inverter, AC Bus 1, Reset, then center, and then back to MDC 3; Up Telemetry, Command Reset then Off, and then select best Omni.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The readback's correct, but let me recheck that.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jack, we were incorrect on panel 5; that should have been panel 3.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Now, when you're in this configuration, the following onboard readouts are required.
Panel 3: Bat C voltage, Pyro Bat A voltage, Pyro Bat B voltage, SPS Helium Pressure.

And the following readouts on panel 101: CM RCS injector temperatures, those are positions 5 Charlie, 5 Delta, 6 Alpha, 6 Bravo, 6 Charlie, 6 Delta; and the battery manifold pressure, which is 4 Alpha.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: You want me to read out on MDC 3: Bat B voltage, Pyro A voltage, Pyro B voltage, SPS Helium Pressure.
On panel 101: Command Module RCS injector temperatures; 5 Charlie, 5 Dog, 6 Alpha, 6 Baker, 6 Charlie, 6 Dog.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: that was correct but I want to recheck the very first one, that was Bat Charlie voltage.
Is that what you read back?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Seems like we're having a difference ...
I can't understand Bat Charlie from Bat Dog.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: that complete procedure for getting into it, as far as getting into it's concerned.
What I will give you now is how to back out of it after you're through with your readings.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Now you'll back out fairly quickly after you go into this procedure.
All we'll need is about 5 minutes of TM, and so if you're ready to copy, here it comes.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Incidentally, copy this down on a piece of scratch paper as I read it, and then later I'll refer back to your first list where you can write things in the righthand margin.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Repeat the one on panel 3 again, Vance.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Next, panel 250: CB Bat Bravo, Power Entry/ Postlanding to open.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: What I just gave you now on the backout is the only thing that's order critical.
From here on, you can go to the steps for initiating the procedure, and backtrack.
In other words, you should go to each of the panels that I read up and put the circuit breakers back to the base-line configuration ...
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: to accent, here's the sequence-critical steps: Power AMFL, Off; Transponder Off; AC Inverter, 2, Off; and the last one on panel 250, CB Bat Baker Power Entry/Postlanding, open; and then hit the steps in reverse order that you gave them to me, and back out, like then I would start on MDC 3 Up Telemetry, Off ...
to Off; and then AC Inverter ...
AC1 Bus 1 to Off; and then AC Inverter, 2, Off; AC Inverter, 2, Main B to Off.
Is that the type of order that you want to back out of?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: just for kicks, let's go through these panels again, and I'll give you the ...
the position, just to make sure there can be no doubt.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: in the area where I said order was not critical, it's still not critical when you back out.
Okay, panel 4: Telcomm, Group 1, AC1, it will not be AC1, it'll go to Off.
Panel 5: all circuit breakers on that panel which were positioned will now come to opened.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Panel 4: Telcomm, Group 1 will go Off.
Panel 5: all the circuit breakers that we closed will go back to open.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Panel 3: the first three lines have been taken care of already in my beginning in the backout procedure, so starting with Mode, Voice, that should go back to Voice.
Power, SCE, Off; PMP, Off; S-Band Antenna, Omni Bravo.
Panel 225: CB Flight Bus, Main B, open; CB CTE, Main B, open.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I'm going to have to switch Omnis.
I'm starting to get some static here.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: How do you read on Aft Omni?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: On panel 3: the first three steps we took care of; Mode, Voice, to Voice; Power, SCE, Off; Power PMP to Off; and S-Band Omni would go back to Bravo.
Panel 225: Flight Bus, Main B, will go open; and CB CTE, Main B, will go open.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The two circuit breakers on panel 250 will go open; the two circuit breakers, panel 275, will go open.
Okay, the circuit breakers on panel 250, we took care of already.
Panel 3: AC Inverter, 2, we took care of.
AC Inverter 2, AC Bus 1 should now go Off.
And the remaining two switches on panel 3 we took care of.
And "Select best Omni" remains the same.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: two circuit breakers on panel 250 will go back to open; the two circuit breakers on panel 275 back to open; the Power Entry/Postlanding circuit breaker, we already took care of earlier; AC Inverter 2; we took care of earlier; AC Inverter 2 to AC Bus 1 will go to Off; and AC Inverter, AC Bus 1, we took care of ...
No, that'll go to Off; and Up Telemetry, Command Reset will go Off.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And I think we're about ready to go with this procedure whenever you are.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: First we got to go through that procedure to power the CSM from the LM, is that Charlie?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: No, this is independent of power from the LM.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: This is purely a CSM powerup so we can take a look at your telemetry and see how cold the vehicle is and that sort of thing.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jack appeared to assume at first that he will perform the procedure with power being transferred from the LM.
A procedure for that was read up earlier to him.
This is not the case for now, with the plan to only use internal battery power in the CM for the purpose.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I won't have any voice with you, so ...
You don't want me to get connected up ...
you want me to just take these readings and come back and tell you what I have, huh?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The comms configuration they plan to use only involves the telemetry system to transmit data to Mission Control, no voice radio.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: we presume, though, that there'll be somebody in the LM that we can call if we have to get word to you.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: There'll be somebody to be standing by, they'll run up into the bedroom and tell me to stop what I'm doing.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, and let us know when you start.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I'm going to start right now, Vance.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Switching watches, and Jack's going up into the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: We have four steps here to give you to request that you get back on Down Voice Backup, if you're ready to copy?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Bit rate to Lo bit rate.
Voice, Function switch to Down Voice Backup.
Power Amps CB, open; Range Function switch in Ranging; that's verify.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Bit rate, Lo; Voice Function, Down Voice Backup; Power Amp S-Band, open; and Range Function to Range; and that's a verify.
And I still have the Biomed on Right here, so we don't have hot mike.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: They configure the LM comms mostly to the low power mode they've been using throughout the emergency phase of the mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And, you want that in work, now?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: I have a question for you, Houston, if you have time,
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And I'm copying down the reestablishing ...
PTC maneuver that Charlie gave me.
Step 5 was, "When rates are less than 0.05 degrees per second, Attitude Control Yaw to Pulse." And just how am I to get my rates down to 0.05 degrees per second.
My rate indicator, at the best, is 0.1 of a degree.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim is wondering how to stabilize the ship per the earlier instructions.
The visual reference available to him for the procedure is the 8-ball, and it doesn't have the same precision as the computer DSKY would have.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim, this has been tried in the simulator by Charlie and others, and what they recommend is that you null your rates on the error needles until you see no movement whatsoever, and then wait another couple of minutes, and that should do it.
That's the only way we can tell.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I'll give it the old college try.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And if you can't attain that, why, of course, just the best you can do.
We'll have to give it a try.
Maybe it won't be quite like the simulator.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The orders are to basically just wait and see, which Jim isn't perfectly happy with.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I've gone all the way through your procedure ...
all the way down to "Select best Omnis," but I'm not getting any Main Bus B voltage and this I don't understand.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: You faded out due to noise in the orientation.
If you close the Power Amp circuit breaker, we can hear you better.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: You faded out at the very beginning, so please go through that again.
We understand you don't get anything on Main B.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Please, repeat back now your conversation.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I don't have any idea where we cut out, Vance.
I just said that we performed the procedure exactly as you say.
We're down into the SEP critical portion to where I said, "select best Omni." However, we're not getting any Main Bus B voltage reading.
I can read Bat Bus B, Baker, at 36.5; Bat Charlie at 37.0, but I have negative Main Bus B readings.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: There's a switch and a circuit breaker that were positioned this morning that we'd like to have you check ...
Main Bus Tie Bat B/C on.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jack is unable to verify the Main Bus B voltage from the onboard display, and they go on checking their configuration to make sure that they have actually enabled it.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're getting data from you, Jack, so that much looks good.
On panel 5, request verification that EPS Sensor Signal circuit breaker is Main B.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: And, we see your Main Bus B voltage at 28½.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: They are finally receiving the much wanted data from the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, Vance, if you're getting data from them, let me go back up there and finish the [Garble].
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I say, if you're getting data from us, that's the important thing.
Let me go back up into the bedroom there and get the readings you wanted, so we can get this stuff powered down.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We've got voltage now, and we'll go back up and take the readings that you want.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'm going back up into the bedroom, Vance.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I'm going back up to the bedroom.
We'll select best Omni and continue on with the procedure.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Reading you loud and clear, Jim.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: And, Houston, I noticed through the AOT and through the overhead docking window that we are venting again.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Understand; the service module is venting.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Jim, would you holler into the bedroom and tell Jack that when he has his onboard readouts that we wish he'd back ...
use the backout procedure and shut down again ...
per the procedure we gave him.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: As soon as he gets his onboard readouts to use the powerdown procedure and shut down.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jack just told me that he's through, and he's going to go through the backout procedures again, the first one he's got is the shutdown.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: "This is Apollo Control at 102 hours, 6 minutes.
We've got 7 minutes, 37 seconds worth of telemetry data on the Command Service Module during this power up of the Command Module.
We powered up just enough to enable us to get telemetry signals and for the flight controllers to take a quick telemetry snapshot of the Command Module.
They appear to like what they've seen.
G and C reports propulsions systems, pressure and temperature all look good on the CSM.
Apollo 13 is 160,331 nautical miles from Earth.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Now back in Earth's sphere of influence, the PAO announcements are made in relation to the distance from Earth.
Right now it stands at 296,933 km and their velocity is picking up at 1,309 m/s.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jack's ready to give you the readings if you are ready to copy them.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The readings that you wanted; are you ready?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Bat Charlie, 37.0; pyro A, 37.0; pyro B, 37.0; SPS helium pressure, 3450; and just for kicks, nitrogen A, 2300; nitrogen B, 2400; CM RCS injector temperatures: 5 Charlie, 4.5; 5 Dog, 3.5; 6 Alpha, 4.0; 6 Baker, 4.6; 6 Charlie, 4.0; 6 Dog, 3.8; battery manifold pressure, 4 Alpha, 1.4.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The values Jack reads out can be interpreted as below.
The RCS temperatures and the battery manifold pressure are read out from the Systems Test Meter.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Pyro battery A and B - 37 volts - normal
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: SPS helium pressure - 3,450 psi - this is the helium used to pressurize the SPS tanks, and is normal.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Nitrogen A - 2,300 / Nitrogen B - 2,400 psia - high-pressure nitrogen gas is used to operate valves in the SPS engine.
This pressure is normal as well.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Command Module RCS injector temperatures - these range from +30°F to +42°F - or -1.1°C to +5.6°C.
They are cold, but have internal heaters that can be used to warm them up before use, hence this is not a critical issue.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Battery manifold pressure - 5.60 psia - pressure in the line that is used to vent the batteries if they produce gas - like the LM batteries have done during the mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: How does the telemetry look on our ...
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The majority of the readings are drawn from the telemetry, of course, rather than the onboard displays.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'll tell you, Deke, it's cold up in there.
I don't know whether we'll be able to sleep up there tonight; it must be about 35 or 40 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's just what I was worrying about.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: Right now, we're getting two sets of CWGs on.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The crew's basic onboard clothing consists of the Constant Wear Garment - a cotton full body underwear, as well as the flight coveralls made of Teflon fabric.
Neither provides a lot of comfort against the cold temperatures being experienced onboard the two spacecraft at the moment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"I don't know how to discuss the clothing and related equipment.
Obviously, the inflight coveralls we had weren't adequate for the conditions we had.
The coveralls are great for a nominal mission.
I hate to imply that we should carry liners or something like that with us.
I sure think we can improve the footwear, though.
I know that Grumman, down at the Cape, has for their checkout people a soft boot that is worn in the LM.
An insulated soft boot would have been much more adequate than what we had."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Haise, from1970 Technical debrief:"With the addition of probably just one set of Nomex thermals, we'd have been in good shape.
We needed a set of thermal underwear on."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Slayton, from1970 Technical debrief:"What did you end up wearing?"
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Lovell, from1970 Technical debrief:"Constant wear garments."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Haise, from1970 Technical debrief:"Two constant wear garments."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's not uncomfortable at all in Aquarius, but it definitely is cold in Odyssey.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We just had a change in watch; that's all.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim, two items: in the Command Module, we wish to verify that the Pyro Battery selector was left in the main position.
And we're ready to have the Power Amp circuit breaker on panel 16 pulled, whenever you are ready.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Fred just tells me that he put it there, and we're checking that again about the Pyro Battery selector in the main position.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Understand that you are checking it.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: And I will pull the Power Amp circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: How does number 2 battery look to you now?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim, it looks like it was probably a sensor problem; the battery number 2 is load-sharing well.
And we see no indications of higher temperatures in the glycol loop or anything that would make us think that it is heating up.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Battery 2 has rallied, despite the earlier issues with it.
The exact cause of the problems is still unknown both to the crew and to Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I just want to talk over a little philosophy here.
Fred told me that at one time you came up and told him that we were a little steep on the entry angle, and now our burn is going to make us ...
I just want to make sure that we're all talking about the same thing, that, in essence, at this particular situation, we're shallow, are not steep, and we are going to increase the angle.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Jim, the situation is that, at the moment, we're a little bit shallow, and retrograde midcourse is going to put us more in the center of the corridor.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I just, wanted to make sure.
Fred had written down, some time ago, that ...
that our angle now was about 71 and we were going to do a midcourse of 7 feet per second because it appears that we're going to shallow it out.
I think we're all talking the same language now.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And, I guess it follows, but your perigee is a little bit high right now, too; so that will be bringing it back ...
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Our readings down here say your LM cabin's about as cold as the Command Module cabin.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: There's usually two people in the LM cabin, and it's a lot ...
It seems to be a lot more compact, and so we don't notice the coldness down here as we do in the Command Module.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Some time ago, I copied down a long Comm midcourse-7 corridor control burn at a GET of 134:59:42.98.
Is that burn PAD still valid?
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: And that is assuming no midcourse 7 here at 105 hours.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: This PAD was for the situation that radio contact was lost and the crew would have to perform the burn solo without ground assistance - which is the essence of a PAD, anyway.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim, we're setting up your burn for 105:30, and we'll be working up a PAD, et cetera, based on that time.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: And, additional point; I guess this one's for Jack.
Do we have any idea why we couldn't read the Main Bus B voltage a while back when first he didn't get it and then later he did?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: I think we have a reason for that.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: It appears, Vance, that the battery charge circuit breaker which appeared to be in, wasn't in.
Fred pulled it and reset it, and then he started getting readings.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: It was the battery charge circuit breaker that allows you to read volts, but we haven't ...
don't know the exact name for it yet.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: The name is Battery Charger Bat B Charge.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: Have you opened your food locker just aft of the LM data file?
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: It came at the right time.
transcript.speaker: Lovell

tokens: This tantalizing conversation suggests that something was hidden in the LM food locker that the crew has discovered.
It is not elaborated on, however.
It is possible that the hidden item in question was a number of gifts from Marilyn Lovell and Mary Haise to their moonlanding husbands.
It is known that something of the sort was found onboard, but we're not exactly sure what.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jim, we have some CSM temperatures here for you that might be of interest, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: Your quad package temperatures range from 85 degrees to 44 degrees.
Your CM RCS injectors range from 44 degrees to 21; and your heat shield is well above its lower limits in all the various locations.
Temperatures appear to be cycling based on Sun angle, and it's no sweat.
transcript.speaker: Brand

tokens: The Service Module RCS quads have a minimum operational temperature rated at 55°F (12.8°C).
Since they will not be used, the low temperature in at least one of the quads is not an issue.

The range of temperatures is more indicative of the state of their Passive Thermal Control, and is determined by how much direct sunlight each part of the Service Module might be receiving at the time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Command Module RCS engine fuel injectors have a minimum operational temperature rated at 28°F (-2.2°C).
This only applies to a dormant system, however, and by activating the RCS heaters later on, they will be able to bring them up to a more comfortable operational temperature.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Command Module has its own RCS jets for attitude control after SM separation.
They are embedded in the structure of the ship and consist of a simple design of a fuel and oxidizer injector as well as a burn chamber with an ablative lining to prevent it from melting.
The jets are helium-pressurized and use the same hypergolic propellants as the SPS engine and the SM RCS.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Apollo heatshield is constructed of a stainless steel plate onto which a fibreglass honeycomb is bonded.
Each of the 370,000 cells in the lattice are filled with the Avco ablative resin from heated caulking guns.
The shield is then cured in an enormous oven to harden the material.
The whole heat shield is then X-rayed to detect any defects in its construction.
The final product is filed and sanded to shape and thickness.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The ablative heat shield has four temperature sensors installed.
The safe range of operational temperature has been determined to be between -150°F (-101°C) and + 200°F (93°C).
transcript.speaker: Commentary
